records.ety: [OE. apprentice, prentice, OF. aprentis, nom. of aprentif, fr. apprendare to learn, L. apprendere, equiv. to apprehendere, to take hold of (by the mind), to comprehend. See Apprehend, Prentice.]

defs: One who is bound by indentures or by legal agreement to serve a mechanic, or other person, for a certain time, with a view to learn the art, or trade, in which his master is bound to instruct him.
One not well versed in a subject; a tyro.
A barrister, considered a learner of law till of sixteen years' standing, when he might be called to the rank of serjeant.

records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Apprenticed; p. pr. & vb. n. Apprenticing.]

defs: To bind to, or put under the care of, a master, for the purpose of instruction in a trade or business.
